sentences.text: First German we saw first thing in the morning.
When the sun came up, we saw them.
They come in with horse and wagon, with the cannons and everything, going through the city.
And then we saw a lot of-- a few German soldiers with black raincoats, which they were the ones who started to organize the ghetto for the Jews.
We are in the city.
We-- we ran out from the house, and we laid in the garden-- from our-- in our garden.
Well, the center-- the city wasn't too big.
Most of the city was maybe one and a half-- one to one and a half kilometer.
It wasn't big.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Well, we just came out.
Nowhere to go.
I think that we went in to-- my father knew some people, which they had a big house and we went in there.
Yeah, Jewish people.
Because most of the Jews were in the city.
I would say 90% of this city-- proper-- was Jewish people.
And we went in there, we didn't have no belongings, nothing.
On the back, yes.
That was in the morning.
And then they started to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] first they sent for the eldest of the people, whatever was left of us, to-- to speak to the Germans.
And they wanted to make a-- a-- like Judenstaat, so people should-- if the Germans wanted to speak, they should have somebody to speak to.
Yeah, and they had-- I think they had five people chosen.
And then the five people made a Jewish police, which they were under the jurisdiction of the Germans.
It was necessary because if they wanted to have 100 people to go to a field to work, they told the-- the Jews which were elected in the-- to run the thing.
And then-- then some people didn't want to go, so the police-- they had arm bands as a police, with a-- with a star-- that they made-- made them go.
We know everybody, because everybody knew everybody at that time.
Well, my father was still-- at that time he was working for people for Ukraine.
People about 15 or 17 miles from the city.
As a carpenter.
As a carpenter, because the people knew somebody in the city hall, and they made him go and do it.
And that's how I am survived.
To go to work, yeah, and come back.
No, the men he worked for, they used to come by with a horse and wagon, and they used to take him.
And that particular time, I went with him a couple of times.
Well, my mother was in the house.
My sister used to go to work in the fields, or in the roads.
Whatever there was to be done.
There was no danger in the beginning, nothing.
We heard that there's a lot killings and pogrom like they can catch 20, 30 people and shoot, because somebody hurt a German, or whatever.
In-- you heard.
Some radios were there, hidden, which people listened to.
And you couldn't do anything.
What?
Before that, no.
Well, it was a pogrom.
This was right before the Jewish holidays.
And I remember being with my dad about 15 miles away.
He was working.
He took me, because I was still young and they didn't take me to work.
So he took me so he can send me back with the men who he was working for, to bring some bread and vegetables home.
Which, when I went with him they never searched the wagon.
And this particular day I set out in the morning to bring some bread and something to my mother, and I saw a lot of people marching from the-- from the field, before we come into the city.
Marching-- Jews.
To the city, yeah.

So the man who-- who I was riding with told me, he said, every time you see somebody just turn the head-- turn yourself away from him, and I'll see if I can do something, because they are killing the Jews in the city.
No, he was Ukrainian.
Were Jews, marched by very few Germans, most of them Ukrainian police.
And they were marching to-- and there were three graves dug, which I find out later, and they were-- they were shooting them and putting them into the grave.
They went in the grave, they shoot them, and then other people went in.
And that's how they killed them.
Yeah, about 3,400 people, or 3,500 people.
They killed them in one day.
Men, women, children, everybody.
From the city.
They marched them, and they told them to take their belongings, whatever, because they're going to work in a camp.
And instead of that, they marched them down to the field, and they told them to undress, and they shot them.
Oh, sure I did.
But I was positive that they wouldn't kill them, because my dad made a couple of hiding places, and he divided family.
He took my mother and my sister and put them in one hiding place, and--
In the house where we lived there, in the ghetto, he made a disappearing stairway-- which it went down in the cellar-- and he camouflaged it like you couldn't find it.
Yeah, and-- and my brother was in another place where they used to hide-- they used to store potatoes in the wintertime.
My mother my sister, Chana, was in one place.
And my brother, Julius, was in another place.
Alone.
And my-- and another two fellows, which was no relatives.
And my sister-- older sister and my nephew were-- run away two weeks ago, because they knew something's going to happen.
So my dad send her away to a place by the name of Mokwin, a small little-- we call it a [NON-ENGLISH], which it's--
A hamlet, yeah.
No, they killed her.
They find her, and they killed her.
In Mokwin, yes.
We went through.
I went-- I saw the people were-- were walking.
Opposite.
All the Ukrainian-- big, you know, the doctors, the pharmacist, I went to the pharmacy place-- they were standing outside and looking.
And every time I saw somebody I turned over, they shouldn't see me.
And he took me out on the other side of the city and he told me, run.
Don't look back, run.
I was 15 years old.
He told me-- because I knew that my sister is in Mokwin.
And we knew that my dad told him if something happens, you should go to Mokwin.
He knew somebody there which they would hide us out.
So I ran to Mokwin.
I didn't know how, but I ran.
I don't remember.
He was Ukrainian.
Yes.
I ran to Mokwin that day.
We're in August or September, one of the--
Of '41, yes.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we-- I ran.
And then somebody saw me running and they started to go after me.
And there was a ditch on the bank of the river, and I went in in the sand underneath, and I-- and--and they couldn't-- they couldn't see me.
And I stayed there until late at night when it was good and dark.
Yeah, late at night until I--
Sluch, S-L-U-C-H, Sluch.
Which it went into the-- to the Wisla.
And I started to walk.
I didn't know where I'm going, but I walked.
And that woman stopped me.
This was maybe 11 o'clock at night-- beautiful moon.
And she said, where are you going?
Are you Jewish?
I said, yes.
I said, I don't know-- I-- I thought that I have a sister in here, which I would like to find her.
Because this woman, I find out later that she used to be-- used to live with a Jewish man.
A Gentile, and she had a Jewish man as a friend.
And she told me, why don't you stay with me until the morning and I'll find out where your sister is.
So I said, fine.
What am I going to say?
Well, at that time, when you're drowning and you don't know how to swim, you catch a straw.
And she made a hole in-- in a big haystack.
The haystack is maybe 8 or 9 feet high, about 8 feet diameter.
And she dug a big hole there, and she put me in there, and she said you stay there and I'll give you-- And she had me there about a day and a half.
She gave me two meals a day.
And when they finished killing, they started--
The Germans, when they finished killing all the Jews down there, they--
In Berezne.
They knew that there's a lot of hideouts which Jews hid.
So the police were still there, the Jewish police.
So the second day they took out my mother.
Somebody-- some Jewish guy, man, thought that if he's going to tell where my mother, my sister, whatever is, they'll spare his life.
So they took out my mother with my sister, and when my mother went down the steps where she was coming, she knew where my brother was, and she says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
What does that mean in English?
Be well, because I'm already gone.
And that night, my brother, Julius, who is still alive, ran out in the middle of the night.
And-- and he went the same path that I was going, and the same woman was there-- the same-- the third night.
And she saw my brother walking-- we looked alike-- and she said, what's your name?
He said, I'm Julius Gershkowitz.
So she said, I have one of your brothers here.
My mother was killed with my-- with my sister.
Taken to the graves there, and they shot her.
[SIGHS] I know because I've been told.
And I know that my father was alive, and he was in the woods after-- about six or eight months after they killed.
He didn't know that we were alive, and one day, they-- he gave himself up.
Because knowing that they killed his wife and the kids, he gave himself up.
Yeah, and they made him dig a grave, because the people told me about it-- were with him together-- are still in Israel now.
I spoke to them in 1968, and they said they know that he was alive with them, and he couldn't stand being alone while his family was killed.
He dug himself-- they made him dig a grave, and they shot him.
He was about 52 or 54 years old I think.
Well, some of them went to the Russian underground-- Russian-- went underground in the woods.
Some of them joined the Russian partisans, and I met a few guys, which they were with him in the Russian partisans.
No, I don't remember the name.
A beautiful face, a nice zaftig woman, and very good natured woman.
I don't know.
But I think that if they find out that one of them harbored Jews, they killed them.
I just told you, Mokwin.
Oh, it's about six or seven miles from Berezne.
Oh, she was in Mokwin.
It wasn't far from river.
She lived almost-- not far from the river.
She let us stay there for about four days.
And then she came down one night and she said, people are talking that I am hiding Jews. ' I would say you should go away.
And we went away.
And she gave us some food and a jacket or something.
And then when we went out there was a big church with the-- with the-- was standing on pillars.
You know, the church was-- there was no cellar, but it was open.
And we went in there-- figured to hide there for a while.
And we met up with a Russian soldier, a deserter.
And he thought that we have gold or money, because he knew that we were Jewish, and I think he wanted to take us both.
Yeah, and then what happened is, in the middle of the day, we started to run.
I said to Julius, I said, let's get out of here because he's going to kill us, or the Germans are going to kill us, or the Ukrainians are going to kill us.
And we went to the-- to the water, to the Sluch, and it's a good thing it wasn't too wide at that place.
And we swam over.
He was shooting with his rifle.
At us, and he didn't hit us.
And when we came out on the other side of the Sluch, of the river, there was the woods already, and we started to walk in the woods.
Yes.
And at night, while we walked, we met up with another six of Jewish people, which they ran away from that pogrom.
And we were there, at that time, for the fourth day we were about, I would say, 15 Jews.
Three sisters, which one of them is married to my brother.
Yeah.
Three sisters, two men, and a couple of married couples.
And then we started to walk, because we hear there is partisans in the woods.
Yeah, and we were-- we started waking for a few days, sleeping in the grass, and finding whatever we can.
We stole potatoes in the-- in the ground.
And at night, we used to make a big hole and make a small little fire to-- to cook the potatoes.
The water was a creek water.
One day I was drinking the water, and I saw little bugs in there.
But if you want to drink, you drink.
And we lived in the woods, and then we met some more people.
By the time-- after a couple of weeks, we were about 30 Jews together.
All Jews.
No, from Berezne.
Maybe a few of them was from a different city, but most of them from Berezne.
Which, we knew each other.
And at that time, we used to steal whatever we could at night in farms.
A chicken, whatever we could.
And we hear that if they catch you-- the Ukrainian people, if they bring a Jew to the authorities they used to pay him with a pound of salt, because salt was very, very scarce in there.
So at that time, they used to pay a pound of salt for a Jew.
Yeah.
Not all of them were bad, but there was some of them which were very bad.
And at that point, we heard that the partisans are not far from us.
And finally, after three or four weeks-- I think four weeks-- we met up with the partisans.
They were speaking Russian.
They were an outfit-- came down from Moscow.

Because when were there for about a month or so with them, I remember we came down-- they made two big fires on each side for about 500 feet, and we had a plane from Moscow come down and bring some ammunition and a doctor, and take some-- they had a couple of wounded people to take back.
They were soldiers, trained as partisans, and parachute down to the Ukraine and to arrest, and do whatever they can there to the Germans.
We were with them for over eight months, until they took a lot-- they took in a lot of people at the time-- kids, women with children-- and they couldn't operate whatever they had to do.
So they took all the men-- able men-- about 12 or 14 of us, and then all the women and kids, and they gave us rifles, each one, each man.
And they said that you should do by yourself, because we can't do, and they-- we-- they separated us, and the kids, and the women, themselves.
And they went their own way until the liberation when the Russians came in.
Now, this was 1943.
This would have, by now, brought you well into the spring of '43.
It was the spring.
To our group?
No.
We had-- the elder-- the older man was the leader.
Oh, the partisan, yes.
The name was Medvedev-- Medvedev.
It's like, a bear in Russia is Medvedev.
And he was a very handsome, nice man.
Was very-- he was very nice.
He was a colonel in the military.
I know, because when he-- when, after the war, we knew about it, because one of the women from our city-- her name was [NON-ENGLISH], who was there, has-- she was very friendly with him.
And when he left she couldn't go with him because they told her that he's married.
But then she went to Russia--
Yeah.
She stayed with her father and two brothers.
Her father died, but they came to Israel.
And I met her in Boston, I think in 1960, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think '60, something like this.
At that time he was a hero, yes.
Yes, I think so.
And he spared a lot of Jewish lives.
Yeah.
Among the Righteous Gentiles, I consider the woman who saved my life, the man who took me through the city while the pogrom was going.
About the righteous-- him, I consider, yes.
Because if he would have wanted, he was the commander, he didn't have to have kids in an outfit like this, where--
Oh yes, he knew.
All Jews, yes, and they had Jewish partisans there from Russia.
There were a few Jews who were Russian Jews.
A little Yiddish, but mostly Russian.
But Yiddish, yes.
Well, we though that Messiah came when we saw them first, because you could sleep, you could make a tent, you could relax, you could make a fire at night, you could laugh.
You felt like you were human again.
They were trained, and they--
Quiet, yes, they had to be quiet.
